ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for chicken bulgogi (FOODON_03315671) but does not specifically represent the rice-accompanied dish; the code is applied as the closest defensible anchor for the chicken bulgogi component.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Korean chicken bulgogi with rice; A0FJP (chicken meat dish) is used as the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Korean rice dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300g, comprising roughly 150g of steamed white rice and 150g of marinated grilled chicken with sauce, as typically plated in a Singaporean food court or Korean restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Korean Chicken Bulgogi (with Rice) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Korean Chicken Bulgogi (with Rice), CR ID F05100066. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Korean chicken bulgogi with rice as a composite dish. GI of 64 is estimated using white rice as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle (GI ~64–72 per University of Sydney GI Database for cooked white rice, Jasmine/long-grain type). The chicken and sauce components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per 300g serving calculated as: (64 × 43.8g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 28.0g GL. This is a high glycemic load per serving, consistent with a white-rice-dominant dish.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 271 mg/100g; a 300g serving delivers approximately 813 mg sodium, representing 35–54% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 1500–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce preparation, avoid adding extra condiments at the table, and limit portion size to manage cumulative daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice contributes 14.6g carbohydrate/100g with only 2.4g dietary fibre/100g; a 300g serving delivers approximately 43.8g carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~28, which is high and likely to produce a significant postprandial glucose spike.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or mixed-grain rice to lower the glycemic index and increase fibre, and should increase vegetable side dishes to slow glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 271 mg/100g and moderate fat at 4.49g/100g are the primary cardiovascular concerns; chicken is a lean protein source and the dish does not contain red meat or trans fat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose reduced-sodium preparation, request grilling without excess oil, and pair the dish with additional vegetables to improve the overall dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Chicken is a lean white meat with low arachidonic acid load; garlic and ginger in the marinade provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols (allicin, gingerols); sesame oil contributes MUFA and PUFA.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may consume this dish with caution if gochujang is used, as chili (a nightshade) may aggravate symptoms in nightshade-sensitive individuals; brown rice substitution is recommended to reduce glycaemic-driven inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Korean Chicken Bulgogi with Rice (닭불고기 덮밥, romanized: Dak Bulgogi Deopbap) is a Korean-origin dish consisting of thinly sliced or bite-sized chicken pieces marinated in a sweet-savoury sauce — typically combining soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, sugar, and optionally gochujang (Korean chili paste) — that are grilled or pan-cooked and served over steamed white rice. The dish is widely available in Singapore food courts and Korean restaurants. The chicken is tender and flavourful with a caramelised, slightly smoky exterior from the Maillard reaction during grilling. The rice provides a neutral, starchy base that absorbs the sauce. Per 100g, the dish provides 132 kcal, 8.3g protein, 4.49g fat, 14.6g carbohydrate, 2.4g dietary fibre, and 271mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 64 (medium), driven primarily by the white rice component, with a high glycemic load of approximately 28 per 300g serving. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium content, high concern for type 2 diabetes due to the white rice glycemic load, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease. It is halal-suitable and dairy-free. The dish is a common lunch and dinner option in Singapore's Korean food scene and is nutritionally comparable to other rice-based Asian meat dishes.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05100066) at confidence 0.95. The record is canonical, reviewed, and provenance-confirmed. GI is estimated from the white rice component using the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source; the estimation basis is clearly documented.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish as of 2026-04-16. Calcium (19.0 mg/100g) and iron (0.5 mg/100g) are reported by HPB and retained. Cultural metadata in the input incorrectly classified this dish as 'western' cuisine with 'western' ethnic tags; corrected to Korean cuisine. Occasion tag 'snack' from input is not representative of this dish; meal context updated to lunch/dinner. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on white rice as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle per University of Sydney GI Database; no direct composite-dish GI measurement was found in approved sources.
